Name: adaptive_information_bottleneck_pruning
Title: Adaptive Information Bottleneck Pruning for Efficient Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically adjusting the pruning threshold based on task-relevant mutual information, we can achieve superior model compression while maintaining predictive sufficiency, offering a novel approach that combines adaptive thresholds with information-theoretic principles.
Related Work: Existing works, such as 'An Information-Theoretic Justification for Model Pruning' and 'Probabilistic Automated Model Compression via Representation Mutual Information Optimization', primarily focus on static thresholds or fixed strategies for pruning. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing an adaptive mechanism that varies the pruning threshold based on real-time estimations of task-relevant mutual information, potentially leading to more efficient and effective compression.
Abstract: Model compression is essential for deploying deep neural networks in resource-constrained environments. In this work, we propose a novel approach called Adaptive Information Bottleneck Pruning (AIBP) that dynamically adjusts the pruning threshold based on the mutual information between model parameters and task performance. By leveraging real-time feedback from model evaluations, AIBP aims to minimize the task-relevant distortion associated with pruning. We will derive theoretical bounds on the achievable compression rates and validate our approach empirically across standard benchmarks. The expected outcome is a method that not only outperforms existing static pruning techniques but also provides insights into the relationship between mutual information and model compressibility, thereby contributing to the understanding of the information bottleneck in deep learning.
Experiments: 1. Establish baseline models on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with fixed pruning thresholds and measure performance metrics (accuracy, F1 score) and compression rates.
2. Implement the AIBP algorithm, using task-relevant mutual information as a dynamic threshold for pruning, and compare it against baseline models.
3. Perform sensitivity analysis on the mutual information estimation to evaluate its impact on pruning efficiency and model performance.
4. Evaluate the models on a variety of tasks to assess generalization and robustness under different distortion constraints.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of accurately estimating mutual information in real-time, which may introduce noise into the pruning decisions. Additionally, the adaptive nature of the approach may lead to overfitting if not properly regulated. Limitations may arise from the reliance on specific dataset characteristics, which could affect the generalizability of the findings.
